title: Ervatamia harmandiana
taxonomy: Ervatamia harmandiana is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as dogbane or oleander. This family is part of the order Gentianales, which also includes other notable plant families such as the Rubiaceae and Loganiaceae. The order Gentianales is part of the clade asterids in the angiosperm phylogeny. The clade asterids is further grouped under the larger clade campanulids. This classification is based on molecular and morphological studies.
uses: Ervatamia harmandiana has been found to have medicinal properties, traditionally used in Southeast Asian cultures to treat various health issues. Its extracts have been studied for their potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. In some regions, the plant is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive problems. Further research is needed to confirm its efficacy and safety for medicinal use. However, its traditional uses suggest potential benefits in the management of certain health conditions.

constituents: Ervatamia harmandiana contains active constituents including alkaloids, particularly ervatinone, which has been reported to possess various biological activities. The alkaloids present in this plant may contribute to its traditional medicinal uses. Research suggests that ervatinone exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. preparations: Ervatamia harmandiana is a plant species native to Southeast Asia, and its herbal preparations are used in traditional medicine for various purposes. side_effects: Ervatamia harmandiana is a plant species that may have various side effects due to its alkaloid content, particularly with prolonged use or at high doses.
